title: Ribollita
subtitle: Tuscan bread and bean soup with cavolo nero
excerpt: A thick Tuscan soup of cannellini beans, cavolo nero, and day-old bread, with a soffritto of onion, carrot, celery, and garlic, a parmesan rind in the broth, and finished with a pour of good extra-virgin olive oil at the table. The soup is deliberately substantial: bread thickens it to porridge consistency, and it is eaten with a spoon from a bowl, not drunk.
sourceNotes: Ribollita (re-boiled) is a Tuscan peasant soup, named for the tradition of making it on one day and re-boiling it on the next, when the bread has thickened the soup into an even denser consistency. It is associated with the contado (countryside) around Florence and Siena. The bread was originally the unsalted Tuscan pane sciocco; a day-old ciabatta or sourdough works well in its place. The dish appears in its current form in sixteenth-century Tuscan household cooking manuscripts.

content.text: Ribollita is not a soup you eat the day you make it, the whole point is the re-boiling. On day one it is a fine bean and vegetable soup with bread floating in it. On day two, after a night in the fridge, the bread has absorbed all the liquid and swollen into a dense, almost solid mass that re-boiling turns into a thick, porridge-like consistency. Day-two ribollita is what the dish is supposed to be.

The extra-virgin olive oil drizzled at the table is not optional and not a garnish: it provides about a third of the fat and flavour in the finished bowl. Use the best olive oil you have, a grassy, peppery Tuscan oil if possible. The soup is deliberately unseasoned at the table with anything other than olive oil and pepper.

items.name: Extra-virgin olive oil
items.prepNote: best quality, to pour at table

items.name: Parmesan
items.prepNote: grated to serve, rind added to the broth

content.text: Heat the {{olive-oil}} in a large casserole over medium heat. Add the {{onion}}, {{carrot}}, and {{celery}} and cook for 10 minutes until very soft. Add the {{garlic}}, {{rosemary}}, and {{thyme}} and cook for 2 minutes. Add the {{tinned-tomatoes}} and cook for 5 minutes. Add the {{cannellini-beans}}, {{stock-chicken}}, and the parmesan rind if using. Season well. Simmer for 15 minutes.
Add the cavolo nero and bread

Add the {{cavolo-nero}} leaves and simmer for 15 minutes until tender. Mash about a quarter of the beans with the back of a spoon against the side of the pot to thicken the broth. Add the {{ciabatta}} pieces, pressing them down into the soup. Simmer for a further 10 minutes until the bread has swollen and absorbed the surrounding liquid. Remove the herb sprigs and parmesan rind. Rest for 10 minutes. Ladle into deep bowls, drizzle generously with {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}}, add a grind of {{black-pepper}}, and scatter grated {{parmesan}} over.

items.fix: Use properly stale bread, at least one day old; press it firmly into the soup so it soaks rather than floats
items.cause: Bread not old enough to absorb liquid, or too little bread
items.symptom: Soup is too liquid and thin

items.fix: Strip the leaves from the central stem, the stems need twice the cooking time of the leaves; 15 minutes for leaves is correct
items.cause: Stems not removed, or not cooked long enough
items.symptom: Cavolo nero is tough and stringy

items.fix: Beans need more salt than you think; the olive oil at the table is structural, not optional. It provides half the flavour
items.cause: Underseasoning or olive oil skipped at the table

items.fix: Use day-old bread; once added, don't stir, let the bread absorb and press it gently; it should swell and soften without dissolving
items.cause: Too fresh, or stirred too aggressively after adding
items.symptom: Bread has disintegrated into mush rather than soft chunks

content.text: Ribollita is the signature soup of the Tuscan contado (the farmland and small towns around Florence). It is one of the great examples of Italian cucina povera: a dish that turns yesterday's stale bread and the leftovers of the bean pot into something better than either alone. The re-boiling that gives the dish its name is a second cooking, not a simple reheat, and it changes the texture entirely. In Florentine homes it is made on Monday from Sunday's leftover bean soup, and Monday's ribollita is thicker and better than Sunday's original. That is the practical wisdom of the dish.
